title: Campaigns With Over 100 Conversions
schema: campaigns(campaign_id, campaign_name, start_date, end_date), conversions(conversion_id, campaign_id, conversion_date)
solution:
-- Conversions per campaign, keeping only the busy ones (>100 conversions).
SELECT
  c.campaign_name,
  COUNT(co.conversion_id) AS conversion_count
FROM conversions co
LEFT JOIN campaigns c ON co.campaign_id = c.campaign_id
GROUP BY c.campaign_name
HAVING COUNT(co.conversion_id) > 100;

title: Average Order-to-Delivery Time per Customer
solution:
-- SQLite version (uses julianday() because SQLite lacks DATEDIFF).
-- For SQL Server, swap with:
--   AVG(DATEDIFF(day, order_date, delivery_date))
WITH per_order AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    julianday(delivery_date) - julianday(order_date) AS days_to_deliver
  FROM orders
)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  ROUND(AVG(days_to_deliver), 2) AS avg_delivery_days
FROM per_order
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY avg_delivery_days DESC;

title: Pass Rate per Course and Semester
solution:
-- Conditional aggregation: count of passes / total students, per group.
SELECT
  course_id,
  semester,
  100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN grade >= 50 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(student_id)
    AS pass_rate_pct
FROM student_courses
GROUP BY course_id, semester
ORDER BY course_id, semester;

title: Properties With Over 50 Views in October 2024
schema: properties(property_id, property_name, location), property_views(view_id, property_id, view_date)
solution:
-- Views per property in October 2024, keeping only the popular ones (>50).
SELECT
  p.property_name,
  COUNT(pv.view_id) AS total_views
FROM property_views pv
LEFT JOIN properties p ON pv.property_id = p.property_id
WHERE pv.view_date BETWEEN '2024-10-01' AND '2024-10-31'
GROUP BY p.property_name
HAVING COUNT(pv.view_id) > 50
ORDER BY total_views DESC;

title: Patients With More Than Two Missed Appointments
schema: patients(patient_id, patient_name), appointments(appointment_id, patient_id, appointment_date, status)
solution:
-- Count missed appointments per patient over the Aug–Oct 2024 window
-- and keep only the ones that missed more than two.
SELECT
  p.patient_name,
  COUNT(a.appointment_id) AS missed_appointments
FROM appointments a
LEFT JOIN patients p ON a.patient_id = p.patient_id
WHERE a.appointment_date BETWEEN '2024-08-01' AND '2024-10-31'
  AND a.status = 'Missed'
GROUP BY p.patient_name
HAVING COUNT(a.appointment_id) > 2
ORDER BY missed_appointments DESC;

title: Customer Churn and Recovery — First Win-Back per Customer
scenario: A subscription / e-commerce business wants to understand customer-recovery patterns. The product team is interested in customers who lapsed (no orders for 90+ days) and then came back to place another order — these are the 'win-back' stories that fuel re-engagement campaigns.
question:
Write a SQL query to:

1. Identify customers who churned at least once.
2. Among them, find customers who came back and placed another order after churn.
3. For each such customer, return:
     • customer_id
     • first_churn_date          -- the order that preceded the churn gap
     • recovery_order_date       -- the order that broke the churn gap
     • num_days_churn_recovery   -- calendar days between the two

Definition: a customer is considered churned between two consecutive orders if the gap is STRICTLY GREATER than 90 days. A gap of exactly 90 days is NOT churn (edge case).
If a customer has multiple churn-recovery cycles, return only the FIRST one (earliest first_churn_date).

Expected output for the canonical test data:
  customer_id  first_churn_date   recovery_order_date   num_days_churn_recovery
  101          2023-02-01         2023-06-10            129
  103          2023-01-10         2023-05-20            130
  104          2023-01-01         2023-04-05            94

(102 never churned; 105 had a gap of exactly 90 days, which is the edge case.)
solution:
-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — drop+recreate the table with the canonical test data │
-- │  so the query reproduces the screenshot's expected output.    │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS customer_orders;
CREATE TABLE customer_orders (
    order_id     INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id  INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date   TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount       REAL    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO customer_orders VALUES
  -- Customer 101: one churn cycle, recovers on 2023-06-10
  (1, 101, '2023-01-01', 200.00),
  (2, 101, '2023-01-15', 150.00),
  (3, 101, '2023-02-01', 300.00),
  (4, 101, '2023-06-10', 400.00),
  (5, 101, '2023-06-25', 250.00),
  -- Customer 102: never churned — all gaps under 90 days
  (6, 102, '2023-01-05', 100.00),
  (7, 102, '2023-02-10', 200.00),
  (8, 102, '2023-03-15', 300.00),
  (9, 102, '2023-04-20', 150.00),
  -- Customer 103: TWO churn cycles, we only return the first
  (10, 103, '2023-01-01', 500.00),
  (11, 103, '2023-01-10', 600.00),
  (12, 103, '2023-05-20', 700.00),
  (13, 103, '2023-12-01', 800.00),
  -- Customer 104: multiple churn cycles, first is 1/1 → 4/5 (94 days)
  (14, 104, '2023-01-01', 120.00),
  (15, 104, '2023-04-05', 220.00),
  (16, 104, '2023-08-10', 320.00),
  (17, 104, '2023-08-20', 150.00),
  -- Customer 105: edge case — exactly 90-day gap (NOT churn)
  (18, 105, '2023-01-01', 180.00),
  (19, 105, '2023-04-01', 280.00);

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Solution                                                    │
-- │                                                              │
-- │  Step 1: for every order, find the customer's previous       │
-- │          order date and the gap in days (LAG window fn).     │
-- │  Step 2: keep only the orders that BROKE a >90-day gap —     │
-- │          those are recovery orders.                          │
-- │  Step 3: number recoveries per customer by chronological     │
-- │          order; keep flag = 1 (the FIRST win-back).          │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH gaps AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        order_date,
        LAG(order_date) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date
        ) AS previous_order_date,
        CAST(julianday(order_date) - julianday(
            LAG(order_date) OVER (
                PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date
            )
        ) AS INTEGER) AS days_since_last
    FROM customer_orders
),
churned_customers AS (        -- 'recovery' rows = orders that broke a >90-day gap
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, previous_order_date
    FROM gaps
    WHERE days_since_last > 90
),
first_churn AS (              -- pick each customer's FIRST recovery
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        previous_order_date,
        order_date,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id
            ORDER BY previous_order_date ASC
        ) AS flag
    FROM churned_customers
)
SELECT
    customer_id,
    previous_order_date AS first_churn_date,
    order_date          AS recovery_order_date,
    CAST(julianday(order_date) - julianday(previous_order_date) AS INTEGER)
        AS num_days_churn_recovery
FROM first_churn
WHERE flag = 1
ORDER BY customer_id;

-- For SQL Server / T-SQL, swap the date math:
--   DATEDIFF(day, previous_order_date, order_date) AS num_days_churn_recovery

title: Three-Day Binge Streak — Find Users Who Watched Three Days in a Row
scenario: A streaming product wants to spot 'binge-watchers' for a re-engagement campaign — anyone who watched on three consecutive calendar days. The team gets a bare watch-log table and needs the list of qualifying user_ids.
question:
Given a `watch_history` table with one row per watch event, return the distinct user_ids who watched on AT LEAST three consecutive calendar days.

Notes:
  • Multiple events on the same day count as one (DISTINCT on (user_id, watch_date)).
  • A streak is consecutive — any gap of ≥ 1 day breaks it.
  • Generalise: the same pattern with `streak_length >= N` answers any streak length.

Expected output for the canonical test data:
  user_id
  501          -- watched 2025-12-01, 02, 03 (three in a row)
  503          -- watched 2025-12-10, 11, 12 (three in a row)

  502 is excluded — they watched on 12-01 and 12-03 only (gap on 12-02).
solution:
-- ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — canonical test data baked in so the query     │
-- │  reproduces the expected output.                        │
-- ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS watch_history;
CREATE TABLE watch_history (
    watch_id   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id    INTEGER NOT NULL,
    watch_date TEXT    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO watch_history VALUES
  (1, 501, '2025-12-01'),  -- User 501: day 1
  (2, 501, '2025-12-02'),  -- User 501: day 2
  (3, 501, '2025-12-03'),  -- User 501: day 3  (STREAK)
  (4, 502, '2025-12-01'),  -- User 502: day 1
  (5, 502, '2025-12-03'),  -- User 502: gap — broken
  (6, 503, '2025-12-10'),  -- User 503: day 1
  (7, 503, '2025-12-11'),  -- User 503: day 2
  (8, 503, '2025-12-12');  -- User 503: day 3  (STREAK)

-- ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  METHOD 1 — Gaps & islands (preferred — generalises)   │
-- │                                                         │
-- │  Trick: subtract a per-user ROW_NUMBER() ordered by    │
-- │  date from the date itself. Consecutive dates collapse │
-- │  to a single arithmetic group; any gap starts a new    │
-- │  group. Then GROUP BY (user_id, group) and count.      │
-- ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH daily_watch AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT user_id, watch_date
    FROM watch_history
),
streak_count AS (
    SELECT
        user_id,
        watch_date,
        julianday(watch_date)
          - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY watch_date)
          AS streak_group
    FROM daily_watch
),
streak AS (
    SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS streak_length
    FROM streak_count
    GROUP BY user_id, streak_group
)
SELECT DISTINCT user_id
FROM streak
WHERE streak_length >= 3
ORDER BY user_id;

-- ╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  METHOD 2 — LAG triple-check (only works for fixed N)  │
-- │                                                         │
-- │  Walk back 1 and 2 rows; if today, yesterday and the   │
-- │  day before are exactly 1 day apart we have a 3-streak.│
-- ╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
-- WITH daily_watch AS (
--     SELECT DISTINCT user_id, watch_date FROM watch_history
-- ),
-- continuity AS (
--     SELECT
--         user_id,
--         watch_date,
--         LAG(watch_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY watch_date) AS prev_1,
--         LAG(watch_date, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY watch_date) AS prev_2
--     FROM daily_watch
-- )
-- SELECT DISTINCT user_id
-- FROM continuity
-- WHERE julianday(watch_date) - julianday(prev_1) = 1
--   AND julianday(watch_date) - julianday(prev_2) = 2
-- ORDER BY user_id;

-- For SQL Server / T-SQL, swap julianday() math for DATEADD/DATEDIFF:
--   DATEDIFF(day, prev_1, watch_date) = 1
--   DATEADD(day, -ROW_NUMBER() ..., watch_date) AS streak_group
